does: This paper introduces a survival analysis framework for evaluating LLM vulnerability to repeated jailbreak attacks, moving beyond binary success/failure metrics to capture temporal dynamics of safety degradation. The authors model time-to-jailbreak as a survival outcome, enabling estimation of hazard functions, survival curves, and risk factors. They evaluate three LLMs against prompts from the HarmBench dataset across three attack categories, revealing distinct vulnerability profiles: one model shows rapid degradation under iterative attacks while two others exhibit consistent moderate vulnerability. The framework generates quantitative measures of how long models resist repeated adversarial pressure and identifies which attack characteristics correlate with faster jailbreaks.
connects: This work extends existing LLM safety evaluation research, which has predominantly relied on binary pass/fail metrics to assess jailbreak vulnerability. It builds on the HarmBench framework and prior adversarial attack studies while incorporating survival analysis—a statistical methodology established in epidemiology and reliability engineering for studying time-to-event outcomes. The paper responds to limitations in current evaluation paradigms that fail to capture how safety degradation occurs over multiple attack iterations. It connects to broader adversarial robustness literature in machine learning while bringing quantitative, time-aware assessment methods to bear on LLM safety evaluation, complementing prior work that evaluates single attacks in isolation.
enables: This framework enables more nuanced comparison of LLM safety properties across models by quantifying vulnerability over attack sequences rather than single attempts. It creates a foundation for modeling realistic threat scenarios where adversaries apply persistent pressure, supporting developers in identifying which models degrade fastest and which attack patterns pose greatest risk. The approach could enable construction of safety metrics that predict long-term resistance, inform architectural improvements targeting sustained robustness, and guide resource allocation for model hardening. Follow-up work could extend survival analysis to adaptive attacks, incorporate multiple failure modes simultaneously, and develop predictive models of degradation trajectories, ultimately supporting more realistic and rigorous LLM safety certification.
